coredata.dc:description: Oxygen activity in highly delithiated LiCoO2 is critical to fully utilizing the energy density of this high-tap-density cathode but still lacks a clear understanding. In this work, we combined the results of several experimental techniques, especially resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) and neutron pair distribution function (NPDF) analysis, together with theoretical calculations to study this topic. Our results conclude that oxygen redox takes place globally in the lattice, rather than forming localized dimerization as previously thought. RIXS results directly reveal the reversible oxygen redox, and NPDF results show that the O–O pair distance is considerably shortened in the highly delithiated LiCoO2. Theoretical calculations indicate that no O–O bonding is formed in LiCoO2, in sharp contrast to the lithium-rich system in which O–O bonding does form. These results provide the rationale for achieving a reversible deep delithiation and high energy density for LiCoO2-based electrodes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Charge-discharge profile and Co XANES for LiCoO2 in first cycle
(A) First cycle charge-discharge profile of LiCoO2/Li half-cell at a rate of 0.1C (27.4 mA/g).
(B) Co K-edge XANES at different charge states, showing little change at high delithiation state.
mRIXS of ex situ LiCoO2

(A) mRIXS of LiCoO2 at pristine state. The mRIXS intensity is indicated by color scale, with black and blue representing the highest and lowest intensity, respectively. Incident energy range covers the typical O K-edge excitations from 524–546 eV. The dashed white line indicates the elastic scattering.
(B) mRIXS of LiCoO2 at highly charged state. The arrow in (B) indicates the fingerprinting mRIXS feature of lattice oxidized oxygen states (see text).
(C) mRIXS of LiCoO2 at discharged state.
Rietveld refinement of ex situ ND data of LiCoO2
Neutron diffraction data and corresponding Rietveld refinements for pristine, charged to 4.6 V, and discharged to 3 V LiCoO2 samples. Structures obtained from Rietveld refinement are also shown.
NPDF and O–O bond distance result for LiCoO2

(A) The relative neutron scattering length of Li, Co, and O. Note that the scattering length of Li is negative and is denoted in blue; the scattering lengths of Co and O are positive and are denoted in red.

(B) Ex situ NPDF of LiCoO2 during the charging process. For clarity purposes, only part of the ex situ data is shown. Due to the high scattering length of O, all the dominant peaks are O-containing atom pairs. The low r peaks corresponding to Co–O/Li–O, interlayer O–O, and intralayer O–O atom pairs are labeled.

(C) Evolution of interlayer O–O pair distances in LiCoO2 during charging. For XRD and ND data, values were obtained by Rietveld refinement of the data. For NPDF, the values are obtained through fitting the specific peaks using Gaussian functions. Results obtained from fitting NPDF data are shown in Figure S6.
Comparison of COOPs computed for LiCoO2 and Li2RuO3
(A) COOPs computed for Li1-xCoO2 in pristine state (O3 LiCoO2) and charged state (H1-3 Li0.1 7CoO2).

(B) COOPs computed for Li2−xRuO3 in pristine state (Li2RuO3) and fully charged state (RuO3). Fermi level is referenced to 0 eV. The COOP peaks corresponding to TM–O bonding states are marked by yellow and antibonding states are marked by dark gray. For RuO3 the O–O bond formation is indicated by the COOP peak with much enlarged amplitude marked by blue.
Comparison of partial charge density for LiCoO2 and Li2RuO3

(A) Sectional view of partial charge density in the energy range of TM–O bonding and antibonding for COOPs computed for LiCoO2 at pristine and charged states. The light green, dark blue, and red spheres represent Li, Co, and O atoms, respectively.

(B) Sectional view of partial charge density in the energy range of TM–O bonding and antibonding for COOPs computed for Li2RuO3 at pristine and charged states. The light green dashed circle represents the Li vacancy in charged Li2−xRuO3. The O–O distance is shown in white.
Illustration of structural difference between LiCoO2 and Li2RuO3
(A) The structure of LiCoO2 showing there is no vacant site in the transition-metal layer that can provide oxygen atoms the freedom to displace.
(B) The structure of Li2RuO3 showing that there are vacancies in the transition-metal layer that can provide oxygen atoms the freedom to displace and further to bond with each other.
Comparison of pair distances obtained from DFT and NPDF

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Pair distance of Co–O, intraplanar oxygen anions and interplanar oxygen anions obtained by DFT, ND and neutron PDF, respectively. For Co–O and (O–O)intra bonds, the bond lengths are centered around the average values as shown in Table 1 with small variations. For (O–O)inter bonds, short and long bonds have considerable differences and only the short ones are shown here.

head.ce:title: Oxygen-redox reactions in LiCoO2 cathode without O–O bonding during charge-discharge

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxygen activity in highly delithiated LiCoO2 is critical to fully utilizing the energy density of this high-tap-density cathode but still lacks a clear understanding. In this work, we combined the results of several experimental techniques, especially resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) and neutron pair distribution function (NPDF) analysis, together with theoretical calculations to study this topic. Our results conclude that oxygen redox takes place globally in the lattice, rather than forming localized dimerization as previously thought. RIXS results directly reveal the reversible oxygen redox, and NPDF results show that the O–O pair distance is considerably shortened in the highly delithiated LiCoO2. Theoretical calculations indicate that no O–O bonding is formed in LiCoO2, in sharp contrast to the lithium-rich system in which O–O bonding does form. These results provide the rationale for achieving a reversible deep delithiation and high energy density for LiCoO2-based electrodes.

LiCoO2 is a widely used cathode material for lithium-ion batteries with high volumetric energy density and tap density. Traditionally, only about 50% of the total lithium is utilized because over 50% Li extraction is believed to cause irreversible reactions and structural degradation with oxygen release. However, there is always a great interest in going beyond x = 0.5 in Li1−xCoO2 to increase the energy density. Therefore, a clarification of the controversial issues related to oxygen activities in highly delithiated LiCoO2 becomes critical. We use X-ray spectroscopy, X-ray and neutron scattering, as well as theoretical methods to study the nature of oxygen activity in LiCoO2 and reveal that oxygen oxidation takes place globally in the lattice without formation of O–O bonds, suggesting little or no oxygen release from the bulk. This provides the rationality for achieving a reversible deep delithiation in LiCoO2.

High-voltage LiCoO2 is an important cathode candidate for high-energy-density batteries. Understanding the reaction mechanism at high voltage is important but remains elusive. Here, a combination of spectroscopy, scattering, and theoretical calculation approaches reveal that oxygen anions are actively involved when LiCoO2 is charged to high voltage. Despite the shortened O–O pair distance, no O–O bond is formed, which favors bulk structural stability and promises high-voltage LiCoO2 application.

ce:section.ce:para:
LiCoO2 was proposed as a cathode material for lithium-ion batteries (LIB) in 1980 1 and used in the first LIB released by SONY in 1991. 2 Ever since then, it has been an important cathode material for LIB. Conventionally, it was believed that only about half of the Li can be reversibly extracted. Deeper delithiation would lead to formation of (O2)n− dimers 3
,
4 and oxygen release from the lattice. 5 Indeed, this has been limiting the capacity of LiCoO2 to 140 mAh/g (corresponding to half Li used) for a long time. However, recent material engineering efforts such as doping and coating indicate that up to 0.7 Li can be reversibly used. 6
,
7 These results indicate it is important to revisit our understanding of the highly delithiated LiCoO2.

Previous studies suggested that oxygen can be involved in the redox reaction in highly charged LiCoO2, 8–11 emphasizing the importance of understanding anionic redox reaction (ARR) in LiCoO2. Recently, a lot of research efforts have been devoted to the understanding of ARR mechanism in several layered materials 12–15 with significant advances achieved. For example, Gent et al. discussed the relationship between transition-metal migration and the typical hysteresis observed in lithium-rich cathode materials with ARR. 16 A further study of theirs used Li2−xIr1-ySnyO3 as a model compound and revealed that O–O bond formation and metal-oxygen de-coordination are responsible for the creation of in-plane transition-metal vacancies and consequent local structural disorder. 17 Another work by House et al. on ARR in sodium cathode material revealed the correlation between local structural ordering, trapped O2 molecules, and oxygen vacancy. 18 Tarascon and co-workers studied the 4d and 5d transition-metal oxide systems, such as Li2Ru1–xSnxO3
14
,
19 and Li2IrO3. 20 They proposed the charge compensation based on the oxidation of O2− to an (O2)n− peroxo-like species using X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) 16 and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR). 19
,
21 They have also reported their results on observation of peroxo-like O–O dimer (short O–O distance of 2.42 Å) in Li0.5IrO3 (delithiated Li2IrO3) using scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM). 20

In additional to experimental efforts, several theoretical studies have also been carried out by different groups to gain insight on key factors that determine the nature of ARR in transition-metal oxides. For example, Seo, Ceder, and co-workers 15 used first-principle calculations to show that the presence of nontransition metals (like Sn) is favorable for dimer formation as the less directional Sn–O bond can facilitate the displacement of oxygen ions, which is considered as a necessary condition for forming O–O bonds. They also pointed out that oxygen anions are activated when there is Li–O–Li atomic configuration, suggesting the necessity of a Li-excess local environment around oxygen. Also, by using first-principle calculations, Saubanere et al. identified the key role of covalency in forming O–O dimers. 14 Their results suggested that a strong covalency between transition metal (TM) and oxygen can stabilize the oxygen holes generated by the oxygen reduction and establish a reversible redox couple between O2− and peroxo-like species (O2)n− which is likely to result in good electrochemical performance. In contrast, a weak covalency would lead to de-coordination of (O2)n− species and ultimate oxygen release as reported by Chen and Islam 22 in their recent paper. This oxygen release is the origin of poor electrochemical performance, in particular the serious voltage-fading issue. 23

Although O–O bonding is typically considered to be the chemical origin of oxygen-redox reactions in most studies, Luo and Bruce et al. reported that oxygen holes are localized without forming peroxo-type O–O bonding during the ARR of Li1.2Ni0.13Co0.13Mn0.54O2. 24 The chemistry of oxidized oxygen states and the debates on O–O bonding formation are critical to the practical utilization of ARR in battery applications. Unfortunately, the direct probe of ARR states in LiCoO2 remains elusive, and debates regarding whether or not the O–O peroxo-dimer is formed in 3d transition-metal-based electrodes remain inconclusive. To answer such an important question, a combination of state-of-the-art characterization techniques is required to probe the lattice structure, electronic structure, and chemical bonding. In addition, detailed data analysis and theoretical calculations are also needed.

In this work, X-ray spectroscopy, neutron scattering, and theoretical calculations are used to study the ARR in LiCoO2 and the possibility of O–O bonding in it. First, the direct detection of the oxygen-redox states in LiCoO2 is provided using the high-efficiency mapping of resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (mRIXS), 25
,
26 which shows experimental evidence of oxygen oxidation in high-voltage charged LiCoO2. Second, we employ neutron pair distribution function (NPDF) analysis to reveal the details of the oxygen-related structural changes. The effectiveness and uniqueness of NPDF in studying ARR have been successfully demonstrated. 27
,
28 To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that these two powerful techniques are combined for studying the ARR in LiCoO2. A striking finding is that, although we reveal clear signatures of lattice oxygen-redox reactions and the shortening of the interlayer O–O distance, the theoretical calculation shows that the O–O bond is not formed in highly delithiated LiCoO2 systems. Our combined experimental and theoretical results provide the fundamental rationality to stimulate efforts in optimizing and utilizing reversible ARR in LiCoO2 for improving its applicable energy density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge-compensation mechanism of LiCoO2 upon Li+ extraction
ce:section.ce:para:
The LiCoO2 material used for this study was synthesized by a solid-state reaction method. The phase purity and the morphology of the LiCoO2 were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and scanning electron microscope (SEM), and the results are shown in Figure S1. The first charge profile of LiCoO2/Li half-cell with a cut-off voltage of 4.9 V at a current C-rate of 0.1C (27.4 mA/g) is displayed in Figure 1 A. During the first charging process, previous reports indicate that no obvious oxygen gas release was detected even when LiCoO2 was charged to 5 V in propylene carbonate (PC) or ethylene carbonate (EC)/dimethyl carboante (DMC) electrolyte. 29 To investigate the charge-compensation mechanism associated with lithium extraction, Co K-edge hard and O K-edge soft X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) measurements were performed on LiCoO2 at different charge states. Figure 1B presents the Co K-edge XAS results. The X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) spectra do not show an evident entire shift upon lithium extraction from LiCoO2, and therefore it is not possible to determine the change of oxidation state solely by using the edge position. The changes in the edge shape, ascribed to the changes in bond length and covalency, can be seen in spectra of LiCoO2 charged up to 4.5 V. Upon further charging, no notable change on spectra can be observed. The O K-edge soft XAS spectra, which correspond to the O 2p unoccupied states strongly affected by the hybridization effects from transition metals, 30 are shown in Figure S2. It is worth mentioning that the soft XAS data at O K-edge was taken in partial electron yield (PEY) mode, which has been recognized to be more sensitive to surface regions of samples. 31
,
32 It should be noted that the spectra may be little distorted due to the difference in atomic chemical environment. However, previous studies have shown the capability of soft XAS data collected by PEY mode in detecting changes in oxygen-related electronic states in similar oxide-cathode materials, even in operating cells. 33
,
34 In our results, all spectra show relatively sharp “pre-edge” features, at about 530 eV for LiCoO2 and get broadened into multiple features in charged states. These features correspond to Co 3d states that are hybridized to O 2p. The broad hump at 535–545 eV corresponds to the O 2p band mixed with the Co 4s/p states. Although the changes of O K-XAS pre-edge features have been widely used in the battery field as evidence of oxygen redox, we have recently clarified that this is not a reliable approach because such pre-edge features are dominated by the changes of transition-metal 3d states that are hybridized to O 2p states. 25
,
35 Indeed, the changes on the O K-edge XAS pre-edge in LiCoO2 upon delithiation levels have been clearly assigned to the changes of Co 3d configurations in previous works. 11 The broadened pre-edge caused by charging corresponds to the new hybridization states between the oxidized Co and oxygen. The multiple peaks are due to the splitting of different Co 3d states that have been discussed in both experiments and theory. 11 The overall enhanced intensity reflects the stronger Co–O hybridization upon charging. In particular, the low energy intensity of the charged electrodes is caused by the lowered Co 3d conduction states after Co oxidation. Therefore, O K-edge XAS has been clarified not to be a probe of oxygen-redox states in this case, 35 and we focus on mRIXS in this work, which has been established as a reliable probe of the oxygen-redox states in various battery cathodes. 25
,
36 We therefore leave the results of the O K-edge XAS in Figure S2 and seek more reliable and direct evidence of the oxygen-redox states in LiCoO2 using mRIXS.

Compared with conventional XAS, mRIXS provides a completely new dimension of information about the energy of the emitted photons at each excitation energy of the XAS process. 25 The realization of high-efficiency mRIXS systems has enabled powerful spectroscopy for studying energy materials. 37 A striking feature around 523.5 eV emission energy and 531 eV excitation energy in mRIXS has been established as a fingerprint of the lattice oxygen-redox states in both Li-ion and Na-ion battery electrodes. 25
,
26
Figures 2A–2C show the O K-edge mRIXS of LiCoO2 at pristine, charged and discharged states, respectively. Overall, all samples display several packets of broad features around 525 eV emission energy with a horizontal pattern. These are features from the Co–O hybridization states, with Co 3d and Co 4s4p hybridization features below and above 535 eV incident excitation energies, respectively. The intensities of these features increase upon charging (Figure 2B) due to the enhanced Co–O hybridization in the highly oxidized state. 57 Furthermore, the Co 3d hybridization feature also shifts toward lower incident energy. This is due to the increase of Co valence in the charged state compared with the pristine/discharged states, which is consistent with the O K-edge XAS pre-edge variation reported previously. 35 It is clear that, while the majority of the mRIXS intensity is around 525 eV emission energy (vertical axis), which is typical for transition-metal oxides and corresponds to the XAS signals, a striking mRIXS feature emerges at 523.5 eV emission energy in the 4.8 V charged electrode, in sharp contrast to the pristine sample where this feature is absent. The sharp mRIXS peak is from intraband excitations to unoccupied O 2p final states 36 which can only arise if O2− is oxidized because O2− itself has no such unoccupied 2p state at all. Therefore, this 523.5 eV feature has been identified as the characteristic peak for oxygen-redox states in many battery cathodes. 16
,
25
,
26
,
36
,
38 It is important to note that the oxidized oxygen states revealed by mRIXS correspond to the lattice-oxygen activities, which is different from other irreversible oxygen reactions such as oxygen release and surface reactions. 26
,
39 More importantly, this mRIXS feature of oxidized lattice oxygen disappears completely if the electrode is discharged (Figure 2C), indicating the reversible nature of the lattice-oxygen activity in LiCoO2. Therefore, our mRIXS results provide clear evidence for the reversible lattice oxygen-redox reaction in LiCoO2 when cycled to high voltages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structure of pristine and charged LiCoO2
ce:section.ce:para:
LiCoO2 was charged to 4.6 V to reach a deeply delithiated state. To understand the phase-transition route during Li extraction, neutron powder diffraction (NPD) was carried out on both the pristine sample and the ex situ sample charged to 4.6 V. The resultant diffraction patterns were fitted against model structures using the Rietveld refinement as shown in Figure 3 . For the pristine sample, the structure has the space group of R
3 ¯
m, with Li located at 3a site, Co at 3b site, and O at 6c site. In terms of oxygen layer stacking sequence, it can be described as ABCABC… as illustrated in the upper panel of Figure 3. Upon charging to 4.6 V, the sequence changes to ABABCACABCBC as illustrated in the middle panel of Figure 3. 40 After discharging back to 3V, the stacking sequence of oxygen layers change back to ABCABC as that in pristine state, shown in lower panel of Figure 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing the structural changes associated with ARR by neutron PDF
ce:section.ce:para:
Since several previous studies on Li-rich materials reveal that ARR is accompanied by significant local structural deformation such as O–O dimerization or TM–Li antisite defects resulting from TM migration, a combination of average and local structural probe is used in this study to understand the mechanism of oxygen anions during deep delithiation of LiCoO2. While neutron diffraction (ND) provides detailed information about the average structure, NPDF analysis offers insight on local structural changes. Taking advantage of the oxygen-sensitive nature of neutron scattering, information about the local structure around oxygen anions was obtained and the results from NPDF analysis are shown in Figure 4 . As expected, due to the high scattering length of oxygen for neutron (Figure 4A), O-related atom pairs make dominant contributions to the NPDF. This is manifested by the fact that all of the strong peaks correspond to O-related pairs such as Co–O, interlayer O–O, and intralayer O–O (Figure 4B). It is worth noting that, because Li has a negative scattering length but Co has a positive one, Li–O interaction contributes negatively to the first NPDF peak and Co–O interaction contributes positively. Delithiation leads to both Li content decrease and structural disorder with the former increasing peak intensity and the latter decreasing peak intensity. These two competing factors result in the nonmonotonic change of the first NPDF peak during delithiation (Figure S3). The contribution to NPDF peaks by oxygen is also revealed by the partial PDF shown in Figure S4A. The contribution of an O–O pair to X-ray PDF is shown in Figure S4B for comparison. The evolution of the shortest interlayer O–O pair distances, which is of primary interest in this study, was obtained by fitting the ex situ NPDF and the results are shown in Figure 4C. The results obtained from ex situ XRD and ND data fittings are also shown for comparison. The complete fitted patterns are shown in Figures S5–S7. The corresponding fitted structural parameters and R-factors are shown in Tables S1–S3. It is worth mentioning that the highly charged LiCoO2 cannot be fitted well using a conventional profile function. The fitting quality can be significantly improved after introducing profile function that describes anisotropic broadening (Figure S8). This suggests that local disorder, likely in the form of stacking faults, may be in the highly charged LiCoO2. 41
,
42 Therefore, NPDF data may be advantageous over ND data in giving accurate O–O distance information. In Figure 4C, the O–O distance information is extracted from the NPDF data by model-free fitting, i.e., fitting the corresponding NPDF peak using Gaussian profile function. The NPDF data is also fitted using structure model with results shown in Figure S9. As shown in Figure 4C, NPDF provides the most reasonable O–O distance. Other diffraction techniques (especially XRD) can lead to unreliable O–O distance (shorter than 2.3 Å). We will show later that the NPDF results also agree very well with the results of theoretical calculation. It should be noted that at x = 0.5, very short O–O distances arise from structural ordering. This is not related to ARR and is not discussed here. From the NPDF results, the interlayer O–O distance is continuously shortened during the charging process, even after Li0.3CoO2 (4.5 V) on the electrochemical curve. If only Co oxidation is considered, this is in contradiction to the Co K-edge XAS results, which show little change after 4.5 V (Figure 1B). However, it is consistent with our DFT calculation showing that charge compensation is mainly undertaken by oxygen redox for x < 0.5 in LixCoO2 (Figure S10). Again, our mRIXS results also directly reveal the participation of lattice oxygen in the redox reaction. The dramatic shortening of the interlayer O–O distance might be caused by the reduced repulsion among O2− anions as they are oxidized. These results are also in good agreement with Bader charge calculation results shown in Figure S10, which indicates that the net charge around oxygen changes more dramatically at a highly charged state. The shortening of the O–O interlayer distance was also confirmed by direct observation on aberration-corrected bright-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (ABF-STEM) images as shown in Figures S11A–S11C. It should be noted that Gent et al. observed transition-metal migration accompanying ARR in lithium-rich cathode materials, 17 which was also confirmed by other independent studies. 43
,
44 The possibility of transition-metal migration in LixCoO2 was also explored in this work by fitting the NPDF and ND data against models having migrated transition metal. In both cases, such an approach leads to either unstable fitting or unreasonable structural features (e.g., extremely short TM–O bond length below 1.5 Å). Based on these results, it is likely that transition-metal migration is minimal in LixCoO2. The difference between LixCoO2 and other ARR material in this aspect might be attributed to several factors. For example, in LixCoO2, there is lack of the Li–O–Li local configuration that is typically found in lithium-rich ARR materials. In addition, delithiated LixCoO2 has delocalized electrons while lithium-rich ARR materials mostly have localized electrons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nonexistent O–O bonding in highly delithiated LiCoO2
ce:section.ce:para: DFT-based first-principle calculations were carefully conducted to explore whether there is substantial chemical interaction to form an O–O bond or not at such shortened O–O distance. An H1-3 Li0.17CoO2 model was built for the charged state based on the structure proposed by Ceder et al. 9 The interlayer and intralayer O–O distances in the fully relaxed O3 typed structure of LiCoO2 and H1-3 Li0.17CoO2 were found to be close to the results from neutron data analysis as shown in Table 1 , verifying the accuracy of the theoretical modeling. By performing Bader charge analysis, the elemental contribution to charge compensation at different delithiation states is calculated, and the results are shown in Table S4. The result shows that up to 50% Li being extracted, Co dominates the charge compensation. After 50% Li is extracted, the O contributes much more to charge compensation than Co, which highlights the consistency with mRIXS results that oxygen participates in redox reaction at highly charged states in LixCoO2.

In order to understand the origin for such shortened interlayer O–O distance, a crystal orbital overlap population (COOP) analysis was performed for both LiCoO2 and Li2RuO3 systems (Figure 5 ). COOP has been demonstrated as an effective tool to provide direct visualization of bonding situation in crystal structures. 14
,
15
,
45
,
46 The COOP results for LiCoO2 in Figure 5A show that the densities of electronic states (black curves) vanish right above the Fermi level in pristine material. In contrast, in charged states, the available electronic states (red curves) expand over the Fermi level, showing the characteristic of electronic structure of metallic phase, in good agreement with the insulator-metal transition at delithiated states previously observed in the Li x CoO2. 47
,
48 The other interesting thing is the big difference in COOP amplitudes between Co–O and O–O (both interlayer and intralayer). The fact that Co–O COOP has the largest amplitude (for both the pristine and charged states) indicates that the Co–O covalent bond is the major chemical bond in the LiCoO2 system, regardless of the state of charge. It is to our surprise that the amplitudes of COOP for O–O (both interlayer and intralayer) are so low compared with Co–O, indicating no O–O chemical bond formation upon charging even though the interlayer O–O distance is considerably shortened. This is very different from the process we calculated for LiRuO3 to RuO3 oxidation shown in Figure 5B, where the formation of the O–O bonds is clearly visualized by the substantial increase in the O–O COOP amplitude, consistent with the previously reported results by Saubanere et al. 14 The partial charge density in the energy range of TM–O bonding and antibonding (Figure 6 ) are also calculated for LiCoO2 and Li2RuO3. As shown in Figure 6A the electron cloud is clearly polarized along the Co–O bond direction with much higher level in the charged state than in the pristine state, suggesting stronger Co–O bonds at deep delithiation. In contrast, only very slight polarization can be observed along the interlayer O–O direction. Besides, the electron cloud overlap between intralayer O atoms is of low density in both the pristine and charged states (the light blue clouds between interlayer O atoms shown in the right plot in Figure 6A) indicating quite weak intralayer O–O interaction. Therefore, it could be assumed that the shortened interlayer O–O distance is mainly caused by the stronger Co–O bond, but not by the O–O bonding. (The absence of O–O bonding is also confirmed by spectroscopic studies like Raman and electron paramagnetic resonance with data shown in Figures S12 and S13, respectively.) This is different from the Li-rich materials like Li2RuO3 as shown in Figure 6B. In Li2RuO3, the electron cloud around oxygen is clearly polarized along the O–O pair direction at the charged state, which indicates the formation of O–O bond.

For Li-rich materials, oxygen oxidation can take place in several forms, including O(2−n)− species with localized holes, 49 peroxo-like species with O–O bond formation, 18 and much shortened TM–O bond with TMLi-VTM defect-pair formation. 16
,
17 In a recent perspective paper, Gent et al. summarized the nature of oxidized oxygen. 50 For all the different forms of oxidized oxygen, if shortened O–O pair distance is observed, it is generally correlated with O–O bond formation, which could further lead to forming peroxo-like species (O2)n−. The (O2)n− can either be de-coordinated leading to oxygen gas release or stabilized in the structure depending on the local bonding environment. In contrast, for oxygen redox in LiCoO2 material studied in this work, the shortened O–O distance is not coupled with peroxo-like species. As the XAS results and electronic structure calculated by DFT indicate, the hole states are not localized in oxygen orbitals. As the fitting results from diffraction data suggest, there were barely any TMLi defects. When oxygen anions are oxidized in LiCoO2, although the interlayer O–O distance is shortened, no O–O bond is formed. The charge transfer is delocalized in the lattice through lattice-oxygen oxidation. 48 Therefore, during deep delithiation, the shortening of the O–O distance alone cannot be used as an indication of O–O bond formation (or peroxo-like [O2])n− species), especially in a conventional non-Li-rich layer material based on 3d transition metals. Instead, emphasis should be given to the much-strengthened Co–O bonds at highly charged states. We note that a strong hybridization effect in 4d/5d transition-metal systems has been proposed to play a critical role in triggering and stabilizing oxygen-redox reactions. 13 Similarly, the enhancement of the bonding between transition metals and oxygen at highly charged states in LiCoO2 could have reached a comparable level and become a critical factor for stabilizing the lattice oxygen-redox reactions in LiCoO2 systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between conventional and lithium-rich layered oxide
ce:section.ce:para:
The finding that O–O bonding is formed in Li-rich system but not in conventional layered LiCoO2, even though they both have shortened O–O distance during deep delithiation, might be explained by their difference in the local structure. The comparison is made between LiCoO2 and a typical Li-rich material (i.e., Li2RuO3) as illustrated in Figure 7 . It is very important to note that in LiCoO2, the transition-metal layer contains Co only (Figure 7A); while in Li2RuO3, the transition-metal layer contains both Ru and Li (Figure 7B). Upon high-voltage charging, many lithium vacancies are generated as lithium is removed from the Li/Ru layer of Li2RuO3, but not in the Co layer in LiCoO2. As recent theoretical calculations show, to form O–O bonding, oxygen anions need to be displaced so that oxygen 2p orbitals are no longer at close to a 90° angle to each other. A large number of vacancies in the Li/Ru layer (Figure 7B) relaxes the restriction on oxygen ions for the required displacement and electron orbit reorientation for the O–O bond formation (sigma bonding, peroxide). Such a large amount of vacancies is not available in the Co layer of LiCoO2 (Figure 7A). Therefore, the O–O bonds are very difficult to form. Since the O–O bonding might initiate the process that eventually leads to oxygen release, 14
,
23 the lack of O–O bonding in LiCoO2 material might be favorable in stabilizing the structure against oxygen release from bulk structure during high-voltage charging. The electrochemical data shown in Figure 1 with high reversibility (225 mAh/g for charging and 205 mAh/g for discharging) may provide some evidence for this structural stability. Such good reversibility was achieved even when LiCoO2 is oxidized to 4.6 V, corresponding to the extraction of 83% Li per formula. (The good reversibility can also be seen from the extended X-ray absorption fine structure data shown in Figure S14.) It is worth noting that the “high structural stability” of the LiCoO2 sample here refers to its reversible structure change during deep delithiation and lithiation, in contrast to the Li-rich layered material which suffers from irreversible structure changes after being charged to high voltage during the first cycle. However, such structural stability of LiCoO2 does not guarantee a stable high-voltage cycling performance because surface instability at high voltage can be severe, as discussed by several recent works. 51
,
52

In this work, we used experimental mRIXS, NPDF, and theoretical DFT calculations to reveal the nature of ARR in LiCoO2 at a highly delithiated state. The clear contrast of mRIXS for electrodes at different electrochemical states provides the unambiguous signature of lattice oxygen-redox reactions in LiCoO2. It is important to note that, compared with other techniques and claims, mRIXS is known to probe the lattice-oxygen oxidation, which suggests that oxygen in LiCoO2 could be oxidized without detrimental oxygen release. By using NPDF, a shortened O–O distance, similar to that in Li-rich systems, was directly observed in deeply charged conventional layered LiCoO2. However, despite of the shortened O–O distance, no peroxo-like O–O bond formation is concluded from DFT calculations, in contrast to that in Li-rich Li2RuO3, where the O–O bond is formed. Such a difference between LiCoO2 and Li-rich layered oxide materials is attributed to strong cation effects, which are different in the two systems. The combined information on the structure, the local oxygen environment, and the electronic structure suggests a completely new picture of the redox reaction and bonding nature of the oxidized oxygen for LiCoO2 cycled with a high voltage-charging limit. Fundamentally, this work shows that the shortened O–O distance alone cannot be used to indicate the formation of peroxo-type O–O bonding. Such O–O bonding is not a necessary condition for oxygen-redox reactions as is generally believed. The absence of O–O bond formation in LiCoO2 is potentially beneficial in stabilizing the bulk structure of LiCoO2 against oxygen release, which provides the rationale for maintaining the reversibility and stability of high-voltage LiCoO2. Our finding of the oxygen-redox reaction without O–O bonding indicates that the oxidized oxygen state is likely associated with the TM–O hybridized states in LiCoO2, because unhybridized states tend to form peroxo species with O–O bond. However, we note that TM–O hybridization itself should not be directly associated with oxygen oxidation. Because if that were true, all transition-metal-oxide cathodes would be wrongly considered as oxygen-redox systems, since all of them have TM–O hybridization. A clear picture of the oxygen-redox mechanism, which could be helped from theoretical interpretation of the RIXS features, has not been fully developed yet and needs to be studied further. Technically, in addition to the mRIXS findings on lattice oxygen-redox reactions in LiCoO2, this work also demonstrates that neutron scattering techniques, especially NPDF, are powerful tools to probe both the global and local structural features associated with light elements (e.g., Li, O), which is crucial for studying the ARR mechanisms in electrode materials. Scientifically, our results on the lattice oxygen-redox reactions without O–O bonding provide new angles for future studies toward the fundamental understanding of the mechanism governing the reversible oxygen-redox reactions in battery materials.
Further information and requests for resources and materials should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the Lead Contact, Xiao-Qing Yang (xyang@bnl.gov).
This study did not generate new unique materials.
All data needed to evaluate the conclusions in the paper are present in the paper or the Supplemental information.

The LiCoO2 powder material was prepared by a solid-state reaction using precursors of Li2CO3 (99%), Co3O4 (99.7%). An excess of 5 mol % Li2CO3 was used to compensate for the Li loss during high temperature synthesis. The starting materials were ground in an agate mortar and the mixed powder was heated at 1,000°C for 10 h in an alumina crucible to form the intermediate products. Then the intermediate products were ground again in an agate mortar and heated for second time at 900°C for 10 h to get the final products.

Electrochemical studies were carried out in Swagelok type cells. For neutron experiments, powder pressed into a free-standing pellet and then assembled together with separator and lithium metal anode in an argon-filled glove box. The whole system is soaked in an electrolyte of LiPF6 (1 M) in EC and DMC (volume ratio 1:1). The samples were harvested from cells charged to various cut-off voltages at the current rate of C/50.

The XRD of pristine material was measured by a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å) in the scan range (2θ) of 10°–80°. The ex situ samples were collected from disassembling the cell at desired stage and washing the electrodes in DMC, followed by grinding into fine powders. Powders were then packed into a 0.4 mm diameter capillary and sealed in the argon-filled glove box. Ex situ powder XRD were measured at Beamline 17-BM at the Advanced Photon Source at the Argonne National Laboratory (ANL). Rietveld refinement was carried out using the software TOPAS-Academic. 53 X-ray pair distribution function (xPDF) experiments were carried out at beamline 28-ID-2 at National Synchrotron Light Source II at Brookhaven National Laboratory using X-ray energy of 66.7 keV (λ = 0.185794 Å) and an amorphous silicon area detector (Perkin-Elmer) to obtain data to large momentum transfer values. Data were integrated using the program Fit2D. 54 PDFgetX3 55 was used to correct the data for background contributions, Compton scattering and detector effects, and to Fourier transform (Qmax = 23.5 Å) the data to generate G(r), the PDF.

Time-of-flight powder ND data were collected on the Nanoscale Ordered Materials Diffractometer (NOMAD) and Third Generation Neutron Powder Diffractometer (POWGEN) beamlines at the Spallation Neutron Source (SNS) of Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL). The obtained powders were packed into a 3 mm diameter quartz capillary for both NPD and NPDF data collection. Typical data collection times were 120–140 min. The NOMAD data were reduced using custom beamline software 47 in a manner that allowed both the analysis of Bragg diffraction data through Rietveld refinement and PDF data.

Co K-edge hard XAS spectra were collected in transmission mode at beamline 7-BM of National Synchrotron Light Source II (NSLS II), Brookhaven National Laboratory. O K-edge soft XAS measurements were carried out in PEY mode at beamline 8.0 at ALS, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory. The XANES spectra were processed using the Athena software package (spectra normalization and background subtraction by AUTOBK algorithm).

The characterization of mRIXS is conducted in the high-efficiency iRIXS system at beamline 8.0.1 of ALS, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory. 37 iRIXS was developed for mRIXS studies of energy materials and is equipped with two slit-less variable line-spacing grating spectrographs with record-high detection efficiency for mRIXS experiments. All electrode samples were handled in high purity Ar glove box and transferred to beamline end station via an airtight transfer mini-chamber to avoid any air exposure. The cathode sample was mounted 45° to the incident beam, and the emitted photon is collected at 90° to the incident photon through the spectrograph with emission energy resolution of 0.25 eV on the O K-edge. 56 Energies in the mRIXS results are calibrated by measuring TiO2 reference samples with the first main peak of O-K XAS at 531 eV, and all mRIXS data has been processed in a 6-step procedure and normalized to incident beam flux, with technical details explained elsewhere. 57

As indicated by the XRD results, the O3 and H1-3 structure were adopted to simulate the atomic configuration of pristine and charged LiCoO2. The H1-3 stands for the hybrid of O1–O3 stacking in Li0.166CoO2 with in-plane Li ordering. All DFT calculations were performed with the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) 58 adopting the projector augmented-wave (PAW) 59 approach. For the crystal structure relaxation, the exchange-correlation functional was taken the form under the local density approximation (LDA) 60 since it has been proved to give a more accurate description of lattice parameters over the whole phase diagram of LixCoO2. 9 For the self-consistent calculation of electronic structure, the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) type exchange-correlation functional in the parameterization of Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) 61 with Hubbard U type correction 62 was adopted with effective U value of 4.9 eV 63 for Co since it gives the proper band gap (2.7 eV) 64 for LiCoO2. For all calculations, the cutoffs of wave function and charge density are 500 and 756 eV, respectively. A k-mesh with the density of one point per ∼0.03Å−3 was generated using the Monkhorst-Pack method to ensure the precision of the calculated total energy. For the relaxations of model structures, the forces felt by each of the atoms were well converged below 0.001eV·Å−1.

To evaluate the chemical bonding status in the system, we computed the COOP between neighboring Co–O and O–O ions in pristine and charged LiCoO2. By generating an overlap population-weighted density of states COOP can easily indicate the bonding and antibonding states. The COOPs were computed based on DFT calculations by Lobster program. 65 In COOPs positive/negative values indicate bonding/antibonding, respectively.
